item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: CPC: THAT DOESNT WORK(LOL)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this on the Manna hub, or did I miss it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My sole issue with this Scip is the implication that all, or even many, people would so drastically change their behavior just by knowing the effects they previously had on others. I understand that's literally the whole basis for the idea, but the article isn't written to imply it only as a possibility. It all but states outright that's exactly what's happening.

I think the research notes could do with a lot less "this is what it's doing" and focus entirely on the parts about how it differs from usual cognitohazards. That could potentially leave the intended idea as a reader-drawn conclusion, while still leaving other, more complex and nefarious ideas, as options. It could leave the reader thinking "Is this a meme so powerful and pointedly effective that it can have an effect on even the most bullish of politicians, or is seeing the harm you do really all it takes to change you? And which is the scarier thought?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice anomaly, very nicely written, and I think presented well—personally, I felt the chill of "this could go badly wrong" when it made researchers try to free D-class.

I wonder whether it would cause sudden shifts in personal behaviour as well? For example, switching to using a bicycle for transport over a car, or changing to a vegan diet.

God, I am with the author on wishing this existed in the real world, though. I suppose it's a fundamentally optimistic viewpoint to think that people only do things which cause harm because they don't realise, rather than because they don't care.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the Magic Kingdom of Landover series, one of the books has the court wizard performing a small bit of magic on the villain that utterly defeats him: he draws out his conscience, which has been so deeply buried that it's all but forgotten. It's an instant and complete victory, as the villain now feels guilt over his misdeeds.

It's a great moment in the book, and this SCP does something similar. It's still great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They are probably overrepresented in the D-Class pool as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It isn't mind control though: showing someone a memory they know isn't their own doesn't subvert their will, especially when the memory is a true one, which seemed to be implied.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's the Capitol building not the White House.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find it ironic that the White House is apparently the central nexus of SCP-3051 activity, while IRL human rights issues are, like, the ONE subject the president isn't constantly publicly contradicting himself on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I finally got to read it, and I feel greatly disappointed by this. Because this does not paint a conpetent Manna — because conpetence is certainly not merely judeged by the effectiveness of the anomaly they use. This again, to me, becomes a case of Manna imporerly utilizing anomlies without a proper understanding of the consequences. The being difference the older Manna skips were Manna messed up already, this is Manna potentically messing up big in the future.

+ So, here's several problems I have with the piece.
- So, here's several problems I have with the piece.

1. The supposedly impactful idea of the skip is delivered to the readers throught the exposition of a doctor's note. This kinda of "the information itself is not anomalous, people are just acting that way becuase they learnt" has defintely been done before, and this is not a particularly good delivery of that.

2. Stating that "the piece of information added is not anomalous" does not change the fact that it's brainwashing. If the information is perfectly normal, there is no way it can't be delivered through normal ways of communication, if it is indeed that impactful.

If the information itself is too vague or abstract to deliver, and the skip is pulling it from someone else's brain, that information is still filtered through that person's perspective, or even with said person's understanding attached. And putting that in someone else's brain is effectively altering that's person's way of thinking, instead of just giving them new information.

3. Note that how some of the Foundation personnel were affected.

We've had seven different researchers break down, two of which tried to free the Site's stock of D-class.

Aftering being affecting by the skip, 2 out of 7 people straight up try to release D-Class, which, if succeed, will no doubt create bigger problems and does not actually help with the situdation. Instead of doing something like advocating human rights or try to come up a better system within the Foundation, the anomaly pratically override their rational judgements and cause them to act without a coherent plan. Even if not everyone is affected this way, 2 out of 7 is a pretty huge number, and these are memetics and infohazards specialists.

This means that there will be a 1/5 chance of people affected by this anomly becoming radical and push forward plans without real knowledge. (These people, with great influence, and was targeted by Manna because of such.) There may well be terrible consequences. This is not even including what if the sources of the information has faulty or incoherent ideas.

The skip ends up kind of being Manna inexplictly getting some high grand cognitohazard, potentially creating a huge problem for the world, despite what they think about it, which is also delivered throught exposition of a doctor explaining this to me.

Then again, all these would just be my personal opinion. I do wish for a more capable Manna tho.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I first read this, I was on a neutral/downvote border. I think this article has the potential to introduce interesting questions, but does not really do so. The whole "is it ethical to anomalously manipulate people into doing the right thing" question is kind of trite to me, partly because cognitohazards don't exist in real life so this is not a question that I have to worry about.

I think my problem is that there are some ethical questions that this thing glosses over. From what I understand, the skip is an empathy cognitohazard: it makes people exposed to it either know or feel-and I think it is a weakness of the article that I'm not sure exactly which it is-what they're doing to other people. I'm inclined to think that it's "know," because of:

takes present, accurate information from other living humans.

This being the case, it's an ethical plot hole to me that just knowing what the impact of someone's evil actions are would compel them to not do it. It seems to rely on a trope of "oh if only people understood what they were doing, they wouldn't do it," which seems suspect to me.

I also was not a fan of the ending,

And honestly, that scares me way more than your run of the mill compulsion coghaz ever could.

because it came off as the researcher trying to convince me that what I just read was scary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would be interested in seeing this idea developed further, into a story that illustrates the anomaly's use and consequences. As it is, it feels like this does the bare minimum to establish the idea, which seems a bit of a waste.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well this gave me one hell of an ethical conundrum. I would lean towards upvote on that alone, but the anomaly itself and the addendum are very interesting.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a lot. It's entirely clear what's happening, but I'm glad you never directly just came out and said it. I also think that you used the different voices and the invented constraints very effectively. I love how ballsy the MCF guy gets due to the treaty, and I adore the coghaz researcher.

+ the hell 1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This puts me in a fun ethical conundrum, because I sick to death of politicians (anyone, really) who don't care about people, but at the same time, I am overwhelmingly opposed to mind control. Despite what the SERD might say on that note.

+ Tangentially related rambling
– hide block

Y'know, it strikes me that this article is indicative of a direction in which the wiki is headed. And I swear, this isn't me telling you kids to get your cognitohazards off my lawn.

Back when I joined up, GoIs were always these nebulous things. Like, you could gather the modus operandi for Wondertainment or the Factory, but what they were, what they represented? Not so much. It was always this shadowy suggestion of, "Oh, this comes from Marshall, Carter & Dark! We've maybe heard of them once before. They're always out there, lurking and watching!"

Compare that to the passage in the addendum where the researcher is all, "Yeah, MCF definitely could not have made this themselves. I'm thinking MC&D, but it's probably above them, too." Like, that never would have happened back in Series II, let alone Series I. It would just be so out of place. But now we're kind of getting into building a world that's more interconnected, where the Foundation is occasionally willing to work alongside GoIs — and this is hardly the first or deepest article to suggest that they would — and I kinda like that. I mean, it's different than what I'm used to, I'll probably never be able to utilize it properly in an article myself, but if the wiki's gonna change, I think this is a change I can support.

So, uh, good job with that. :B Or something. Ya darn kids.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I definitely considered it, but I figured the relatively small sample size and the sheer shock of that much information intake should make the number fairly negligible. Maybe I'll add a footnote for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Might include something about a small percentage of people being resistant or immune to the scip's effects, to account for people on the "functional psychopath" spectrum. They tend to show up a lot in business and government, since they like being in charge: Wikipedia gives a cited statistic of 3-4% compared to 1% in the general population.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A coghaz that does not magically alter views but rather present ideas that make the exposed alter their own views, that's quite a refreshing approach to coghazs and a clever part on the MCF.

+1 with enthusiasm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed, it's a pity they haven't been used much since Series 2. Btw, if you have lingering criticisms I'd gladly listen to them to improve the quality of the piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whales are mammals. Manna sounds similar to mammal. Coincidence? I think not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like anything tying in MCF. The idea of an organization out there solely devoted to affecting good while other organizations do the opposite is cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Manna Charitable Foundation vs the Foundation. Battle of architecture.

I'm so sorry…

Anyway! Great article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Headcanon: The whales were behind this all along. Glory be to our Manna Whale overlords.

Also, good scip +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really cool. Pretty good writing and I like all the extra implication stuff in there (the accord, origin of the meme, etc.).

On a completely unrelated note, I'm going to save some whales.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite like this. It's nice seeing MCF actually accomplishing something they set out to do with their SCPs for once (without immediate catastrophic side effects). It's just the right length, too, and is nice and direct without walloping you over the head with its message.

item.title: Author's Note
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to Spookypizza, Maliceaforethought, Lexinator, and Gaffney for crit on this. Also thanks to DrMagnus for the amazing department report formatting code. If you enjoyed, feel free to check out my other stuff here: http://www.scp-wiki.net/modern-erasmus-author-page

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Aerial_view_of_the_Capitol_Hill.jpg
https://openclipart.org/detail/122833/all-seeing-tech-eye
Images are both public domain

As someone who works pro bono as a human rights lobbyist irl, this is straight from my own frustrations. I've wanted so many times to really show people the suffering their actions are causing, in the belief that if they only really knew then they'd change. Considering Manna doesn't exactly have the restrictions a normal ngo does, I believe it only follows that they'd eventually act on the same frustration.
